title: Theory of Magnetic Fields
narrationScript: Magnetic particle testing finds cracks by making a part magnetic and watching where that magnetism escapes. So before we touch a yoke or a bench unit, we need to understand magnetic fields themselves. Where they come from, what they look like, and how they behave around magnetized objects. That is this lesson. Start with the field idea. A magnet does not need to touch a steel washer to pull it in. The space around the magnet is changed, and we describe that changed space as a magnetic field. To work with fields we draw them as lines of flux. The lines are imaginary, but they follow real rules. Every line is a closed loop. Outside a bar magnet the lines travel from the north pole to the south pole, and inside the metal they continue from south back to north, completing the loop. Lines never cross each other. They crowd together where the field is strong, at the poles, and spread apart where it is weak. And given a choice, flux prefers an easy path. It would rather travel through iron or steel than through air, the same way current prefers copper over rubber. Hold those rules, because almost everything in this course leans on them. The oldest field you work beside is the one under your feet. The earth behaves like a giant, slightly tilted bar magnet, and its field at the surface is weak, about half a gauss in most places. A compass needle is just a small magnet pivoting freely until it lines up with that field. Half a gauss sounds like nothing, and for magnetizing parts it is. But it matters to us in two ways. First, when we demagnetize a part after testing, a common acceptance point is three gauss or less on a field indicator, and you should know the instrument is sitting in roughly half a gauss of natural background the whole time. Second, long steel parts stored or machined while lying north to south can slowly pick up a weak magnetization from the earth itself, which is one reason some shops deliberately rack long bars east to west. The earth's field is small, but it never turns off. Now the discovery that makes our whole method possible. Electric current creates a magnetic field. Pass current through any conductor and a magnetic field instantly surrounds it, in circles around the conductor, at right angles to the current flow. There is a simple way to keep the direction straight. Grip the conductor with your right hand, thumb pointing the way conventional current flows, and your curled fingers show the direction the flux circles. More current means a stronger field, and the field is strongest right at the conductor surface, falling off as you move away. Reverse the current and the field reverses with it. And the conductor does not have to be steel. Copper carries the same circular field, which is exactly how a central conductor will let us magnetize hollow parts later in the course. Wind that same conductor into a coil and something useful happens. The circular fields of all the turns add together, and inside the coil they combine into one strong field running straight down the axis of the coil. Put a part inside and the flux runs along the length of the part. So remember the two geometries. A straight conductor gives a circular field around itself. A coil gives a longitudinal field along its axis. Every magnetizing technique you will ever use is one of those two. Finally, look at the patterns around magnetized objects. A bar magnet has two poles, and flux leaves and enters the part at those poles, which is exactly where iron particles cling. Bend the bar into a horseshoe and the poles face each other, concentrating a strong, useful field in the gap between them. That is the geometry of a yoke. Now bend it further until the ends fuse into a closed ring. The poles vanish. The flux circulates inside the ring, almost nothing escapes, and the ring will not pick up so much as a paper clip. But saw a thin slot through that ring and two poles instantly appear at the cut, with flux bulging out across the gap to bridge it. A crack does the same thing. That is the entire principle of magnetic particle testing, seen in a ring magnet. A flaw that interrupts flux creates poles. Poles leak flux. And leaking flux grabs particles. Keep that picture in your head, because the rest of this course is just learning how to create the right field, in the right direction, and how to read what the particles tell us.
imagePrompt: A technician in safety glasses holds a pocket compass beside a long steel shaft on a workbench while a horseshoe magnet, loose steel washers, and a coiled magnetizing cable rest nearby under bright shop lighting.

sections.heading: Fields and Lines of Flux
sections.body:
A magnet changes the space around it. Bring a steel washer near a magnet and it is pulled in before they ever touch — something real occupies that gap. We call it a **magnetic field**, and to work with it we draw imaginary **lines of flux** that map its shape and strength.

Flux lines obey rules you will use on every shift of MT work:

- Each line is a **closed loop**. Outside a bar magnet the line runs from the north pole to the south pole; inside the metal it continues from south back to north. A flux line never starts or stops.
- Lines **never cross** one another.
- Lines crowd together where the field is strong — at the poles — and spread apart where it is weak. That crowding is what we will later quantify as flux density.
- Flux takes the **easiest available path**. It travels far more willingly through ferromagnetic metal than through air, the way current favors copper over an insulator.

Hold onto these rules. The behavior of every yoke, coil, and head shot in this course — and every indication you will ever interpret — traces straight back to them.

sections.heading: The Earth's Magnetic Field
sections.body:
The planet itself is the oldest magnet you work beside. The earth behaves like an enormous bar magnet tilted slightly off its spin axis, with flux looping from pole to pole. At the surface the field is weak — roughly **0.5 gauss** in most locations. A compass is nothing more than a small pivoting magnet aligning itself with that field.

Half a gauss will not magnetize a part, but it earns its place in MT training for two reasons:

- **Background for instruments.** When you verify demagnetization with a pocket field indicator, a common procedure limit is on the order of **3 gauss or less** — measured by an instrument sitting in about half a gauss of natural background the entire time. Knowing that floor exists keeps low readings in perspective.
- **Slow magnetization of stored stock.** Long steel parts stored, machined, or repeatedly handled while aligned north–south can gradually acquire a weak longitudinal field from the earth alone, which is why some shops deliberately rack long bars east–west.

The takeaway: magnetic fields are everywhere, all the time. MT simply creates one deliberately — in a chosen direction, thousands of times stronger.

sections.heading: Current Makes a Field
sections.body:
In 1820, Hans Christian Ørsted noticed a compass needle jump whenever a nearby wire carried current. That observation is the foundation of every MT machine ever built: **electric current always creates a magnetic field**.

Around a straight conductor the field forms **concentric circles** centered on the conductor, lying at right angles to the current. To find its direction, use the **right-hand rule**: grip the conductor with your right hand, thumb pointing the way conventional current flows, and your curled fingers point the way the flux circles. Reverse the current and the field reverses. Three more facts matter:

- Field strength is **proportional to current** — more amperes, more flux.
- The field is **strongest at the conductor surface** and weakens with distance.
- The conductor itself does **not** need to be ferromagnetic — copper carries the same circular field, which is exactly how a central conductor magnetizes a hollow steel part from the inside.

Wind the conductor into a coil and the circular fields of the turns add together: inside the coil they merge into one strong **longitudinal field running along the coil axis**. A straight conductor gives a circular field; a coil gives a longitudinal field. Every magnetizing technique in this course is one of those two geometries.

sections.caption: Concentric flux circles around a straight current-carrying conductor (right-hand rule), and the longitudinal field produced along the axis of a coil.

sections.heading: Patterns Around Magnetized Materials
sections.body:
Now look at the field a magnetized part carries.

A **bar magnet** is the simplest case: flux exits at the north pole, loops through the air, re-enters at the south pole, and completes the circuit through the metal. The poles are where flux crosses the surface — and the poles are where iron particles cling. File that fact away.

Bend the bar into a **horseshoe** and the two poles face each other across a gap. The flux concentrates in that gap, creating a strong, useful working field between the poles. An MT **yoke** is exactly this geometry, built as an electromagnet so you can switch the field on and off.

Now close the horseshoe completely into a **ring**. The poles disappear. Flux circulates contentedly inside the metal — a closed, low-reluctance loop — and almost none escapes. A defect-free magnetized ring will not attract a single particle, because with no poles there is no external field.

Cut a thin slot through that ring and everything changes: two fresh poles appear at the faces of the cut, and flux bulges out across the gap to bridge it. A crack does precisely the same thing on a smaller scale. That bulge of escaping flux is called a **leakage field**, and it is the entire physical basis of magnetic particle testing.

sections.caption: Flux patterns around a bar magnet and a horseshoe magnet — flux exits at north, enters at south, and concentrates in the horseshoe gap.

sections.title: A crack makes poles
sections.body: A closed magnetized ring shows nothing — its flux is contained. Slot the ring and poles appear at the cut, flux leaks out, and particles pile on. Every MT indication you will ever see is this exact event in miniature: **a discontinuity interrupts flux, creates poles, and the leakage field traps particles.**

sections.caption: A closed magnetized ring keeps its flux inside; a slotted ring forms poles at the cut and throws a leakage field that traps particles.

figures.title: Field patterns: bar and horseshoe magnets
Field around a current-carrying conductor and coil

interactive.title: Find the Leakage Field
interactive.intro: A closed magnetized ring and a slotted magnetized ring sit side by side, and magnetic particles are dusted over both. Click the one location where particles will build up.

regions.label: Slot in the right-hand ring
regions.feedback: Correct. The slot interrupts the circulating flux, poles form on each face of the cut, and flux leaks out across the gap — that local leakage field is what attracts and holds particles.

regions.label: Top of the closed ring
regions.feedback: No buildup here. The closed ring keeps all of its flux inside the metal — no poles, no leakage, nothing for particles to hold onto.

regions.label: Bore of the closed ring
regions.feedback: The bore is just air inside a closed flux loop. With no break in the metal, there is no leakage field here either.

regions.label: Sound metal opposite the slot
regions.feedback: Away from the slot the flux is still flowing inside the metal, so the surface stays clean. Leakage appears only where a discontinuity interrupts the flux.

checkQuestions.question: The strength of the earth's magnetic field at the surface is approximately:
options: 30 gauss
0.5 gauss
1,000 gauss
1 tesla
checkQuestions.explanation: The earth's surface field is roughly half a gauss — far too weak to magnetize parts, but real background context when you verify demagnetization to a typical limit such as 3 gauss or less.

checkQuestions.question: Current flows through a straight copper bar. The magnetic field around the bar:
options: Runs parallel to the bar, in the direction of current flow
Points radially outward from the bar's surface
Forms concentric circles around the bar, at right angles to the current
Does not exist, because copper is not ferromagnetic
checkQuestions.explanation: Current always produces a circular field around the conductor — right-hand rule: thumb along the current, fingers curl with the flux. The conductor need not be ferromagnetic, which is why a copper central conductor can magnetize a hollow steel part.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about magnetic lines of flux is correct?
options: They form closed loops and never cross one another
They begin at the north pole and terminate permanently at the south pole
They intersect at the poles, where the field is strongest
They prefer air over steel because air offers less reluctance
checkQuestions.explanation: Flux lines are continuous closed loops — outside the magnet from north to south, returning inside the metal from south to north — and they never cross. They favor low-reluctance paths, which means steel over air.

checkQuestions.question: A defect-free, circularly magnetized ring attracts no magnetic particles because:
options: Ring shapes cannot hold a magnetic field
The flux is entirely contained within the metal — no poles, so no external leakage field
The field cancels itself to zero everywhere, inside and out
The particles are repelled by the bore
checkQuestions.explanation: A closed ring is a complete low-reluctance flux loop: strong flux circulates inside, but with no poles there is no external field to attract particles. Cut the ring and poles instantly appear at the gap.

checkQuestions.question: A part is magnetized in a coil, so flux runs along its length. Which discontinuity orientation produces the strongest leakage field?
options: Longitudinal — parallel to the part axis
Any orientation produces equal leakage
None — coil fields cannot produce leakage
Transverse — perpendicular to the part axis
checkQuestions.explanation: Leakage is strongest when flux crosses a discontinuity at 90 degrees and remains useful down to about 45 degrees. A coil's longitudinal field therefore finds transverse flaws; longitudinal flaws need a circular field — which is why full coverage takes two roughly perpendicular magnetizations.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §2.1 (theory of magnetic fields)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §2.1.1–2.1.3 (earth's field, electrical fields, fields around magnetized materials)
Practices such as ASTM E709 describe magnetic field principles applied to MT (generic reference)
Reference value: earth's surface field ≈ 0.5 gauss
